title: Brief, daily meditation enhances attention, memory, mood, and emotional regulation in non-experienced meditators
headline: Brief, daily meditation enhances attention, memory, mood, and emotional regulation in non-experienced meditato
summary: A randomized controlled trial of mindfulness / meditation examined focus & attention. Compared to our control group, we found that 8 but not 4 weeks of brief, daily meditation decreased negative mood state and enhanced attention, working memory, and recognition memory as well as decreased state anxiety scores on the TSST. This study not only suggests a lower limit for the duration of brief daily meditation needed to see significant benefits in non-experienced meditators, but suggests that even relatively short daily meditation practice can have similar behavioral effects as longer duration and higher-intensity mediation practices.
keyFindings: Compared to our control group, we found that 8 but not 4 weeks of brief, daily meditation decreased negative mood state and enhanced attention, working memory, and recognition memory as well as decreased state anxiety scores on the TSST.

This study not only suggests a lower limit for the duration of brief daily meditation needed to see significant benefits in non-experienced meditators, but suggests that even relatively short daily meditation practice can have similar behavioral effects as longer duration and higher-intensity mediation practices.
limitations: Meditation is an ancient practice that cultivates a calm yet focused mind; however, little is known about how short, practical meditation practices affect cognitive functioning in meditation-naïve populations.

This study not only suggests a lower limit for the duration of brief daily meditation needed to see significant benefits in non-experienced meditators, but suggests that even relatively short daily meditation practice can have similar behavioral effects as longer duration and higher-intensity mediation practices.
Single-trial result; requires replication, and self-reported outcomes may inflate apparent effects.

The practice studied (mindfulness / meditation) is not the Miracle of Mind practice, so its findings don't transfer directly — though the shared directed-attention component may make some transfer plausible (see “Why this matters for the 7-minute practice” below).
simplifiedTitle: Brief, daily meditation enhances attention, memory, mood, and emotional regulation in non-experienced meditato
aiSnippet: RCT, Behavioural brain research (2019). Compared to our control group, we found that 8 but not 4 weeks of brief, daily meditation decreased negative mood state and enhanced attention, working memory, and recognition memory as well as decreased state anxiety scores on the TSST. This study not only suggests a lower limit for the duration of brief daily meditation needed to see significant benefits in non-experienced meditators, but suggests that even relatively short daily meditation practice can have similar behavioral effects as longer duration and higher-intensity mediation practices.
whyItMatters: MoM shares directed attention (the focused-attention component) in a brief, guided format with the practice studied here. Because the benefit is linked to that mechanism rather than to any one method, it's reasonable to expect it may extend to MoM as well — though MoM hasn't been studied directly for this outcome.
